figures.caption: Fig. 1. A single arm uses press-and-slide to transport an object, then leverages it as a tool to reposition a second object.
Fig. 8. Single-arm Planar Pushing. The same box is directed to the same target, using different contact points (Top: Left face, Bottom: Rear face).
Fig. 11. Unicycle point distance over time and tracking performance. Employing a virtual target, the desired one can be reached.
Fig. 9. Comparison of tracking errors during single-arm planar pushing. Having an arm tracking term, the path is tracked faster and with less error.
Fig. 10. Top press-and-slide motion. The box is controlled as a unicycle object, achieving desired position and orientation with a virtual target ahead of the real target.
Fig. 2. Kinematic abstractions of single-arm rear pushing. (a) Symmetric pushing yields a Rear-Wheel Steering (RWS) model. (b) A lateral offset introduces a bias, distorting the steering envelope.
Fig. 3. Single-arm top press-and-slide. The virtual axle lies on the longitudinal axis for symmetric contacts (a) and diametrically opposite the CoM for unsymmetric contacts (b).

Fig. 12. Bimanual Pivot and Move. Left arm presses the box to the ground, bringing the center of pressure close to itself. After the right arm rotates the box, the left arm moves it forward to its slot.
Fig. 13. Bimanual Press-Fit. The arms first press the object to the wall and rotate, and then they press-fit it into the window on the wall.

Fig. 4. Top press-and-slide motion admits a planar force-invariant, body-fixed tracking point. This geometric point remains kinematically valid for all applied planar forces within the 2D friction circle; and acts like a virtual axle, allowing motion planning as a unicycle, car, or forklift.
Fig. 5. Dual-arm rear pushing collapses into an Equivalent Bicycle model (a) via synchronized forces, or a Differential Drive model (b) via pure differential normal forces.
Fig. 6. Orthogonal bimanual pushing generates bifurcated unicycle regions depending on rotation direction, causing the ICR to teleport during motion transitions.
Fig. 7. Dual-arm top press-and-slide. The CoP is bounded within the triangular convex hull (a). Shifting the normal load allows extreme quasiholonomic maneuvers like single-contact pivoting (b).
